title: Heng-Hsieh Chang
text:
Marketing professional with 6+ years of experience driving market expansion and revenue growth across digital and brand management roles. Currently advancing my analytical capabilities as a Master of Business Analytics student at Monash University, I’m passionate about bridging creative strategy with data-driven insights.
I specialize in developing data-informed marketing strategies that integrate analytics, SEO/SEM optimization, and compelling storytelling to maximize ROI and strengthen client relationships.
My passion lies in turning complex datasets into actionable insights and impactful narratives that support strategic decision-making.


🔧 What I Bring
A unique blend of marketing strategy expertise and advanced analytics skills to solve complex business challenges and deliver measurable growth.



💼 Professional Experience
Brand Manager | flyingV Crowdfunding Platform
2021 - 2024
Digital Marketer | Taiwan Rakuten Ichiba
2019 - 2021



🌱 Beyond Work
I enjoy exploring new cuisines, singing, and playing badminton. I believe creativity thrives where curiosity meets connection — both in data and in life.

title: How Climate Change Fueled Australia’s Black Summer
text:
In the summer of 2019–2020, Australia faced one of the most devastating bushfires in history — the “Black Summer”. Over 30 lives were lost, thousands of homes were destroyed, and an estimated 1.5 billion animals perished.
But was this catastrophic event just bad luck — or a symptom of a warming planet? In this post, we dig into data from a 2021 to uncover how human-driven extreme weather is fueling Australia’s bushfire crisis and what it means for our future.

title: How Climate Change Fueled Australia’s Black Summer
text:
Building a Fire-Resilient Future
The numbers tell a clear story: the “Black Summer” bushfires are a sign of a new, climate-driven reality. Figure Figure 1 shows that extreme fire weather is at least 30% more likely, and heatwaves are twice as common, because of climate change. If global warming reaches 2°C, these fires could become 4 to 8 times more frequent. As van Oldenborgh et al. (2021) warn, we’re likely underestimating the risks. To stay safe, Australia needs stronger firefighting resources, smarter urban planning, and bold climate policies. You can help by supporting local fire prevention efforts or advocating for climate action in your community.

title: How Climate Change Fueled Australia’s Black Summer
text:
References
van Oldenborgh, G. J., Krikken, F., Lewis, S., et al. (2021). Attribution of the Australian bushfire risk to anthropogenic climate change. Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences, 21, 941–962. https://doi.org/10.5194/nhess-21-941-2021

title: How Climate Change Fueled Australia’s Black Summer
text:
In the summer of 2019–2020, Australia faced one of the most devastating bushfires in history — the “Black Summer”. Over 30 lives were lost, thousands of homes were destroyed, and an estimated 1.5 billion animals perished.
But was this catastrophic event just bad luck — or a symptom of a warming planet? In this post, we dig into data from a 2021 to uncover how human-driven extreme weather is fueling Australia’s bushfire crisis and what it means for our future.

title: How Climate Change Fueled Australia’s Black Summer
text:
The Data Behind the Flames
The study by van Oldenborgh et al. (2021) , published in Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences. It uses long-term climate observations dating back to 1900, modern atmospheric data (ERA5), and 11 global climate models to measure how climate change has increased the risk of extreme bushfires in southeastern Australia.
They used two main indicators of bushfire danger:



Table 1










Metric
Description
Data Source




Fire Weather Index (FWI)
Combines temperature, humidity, wind, and rainfall to estimate fire danger.
ERA5 reanalysis (1979–2020)


Monthly Severity Rating (MSR)
Measures how long and severe a fire event lasts.
Australian Bureau of Meteorology






Table 1 shows key fire risk metrics used in the study.
Using these metrics, the authors estimated how the probability of extreme fire weather has changed in a warming world. Their models compared the actual climate to a hypothetical world without human-caused emissions.

title: How Climate Change Fueled Australia’s Black Summer
text:
What They Found

Fire Weather Is 30% More Likely
The study found that climate change has made extreme fire weather events at least 30% more frequent. For a 2 °C warmer world, that frequency could rise 4 – 8 times higher.









Figure 1: Probability ratio of extreme fire weather under different climate scenarios. Climate change has increased the likelihood of such events by at least 30% since pre-industrial times, and the risk could quadruple in a 2°C warmer world (simplified from van Oldenborgh et al., 2021).






Extreme Heat Is the Main Driver
The increase in risk is strongly linked to record-breaking heatwaves rather than rainfall deficits. Figure 1 shows how the probability of extreme high-temperature events has doubled since 1900.
